narrative: TP53 (tumor protein p53) is the most frequently mutated gene in human cancers, with R248W being one of the six hotspot mutations in the DNA-binding domain. This gain-of-function mutation disrupts DNA binding and confers oncogenic properties. R248W is found in approximately 7.2% of TP53-mutant cancers.

drugs.name: APR-246 (Eprenetapopt)
drugs.mechanism: Reactivates mutant p53 by restoring wild-type conformation

drugs.name: PRIMA-1
drugs.mechanism: Converts to methylene quinuclidinone, binds cysteine residues in p53 core domain

drugs.name: Arsenic Trioxide
drugs.mechanism: Stabilizes p53 folding via zinc coordination

key_findings: R248W p53 exhibits gain-of-function oncogenic activity beyond loss of tumor suppression
Structural studies reveal R248W disrupts DNA minor groove interactions
Combination of APR-246 with cisplatin shows synergistic effects in ovarian cancer
Cryo-EM structures reveal allosteric mechanisms of p53 reactivation compounds
Machine learning models predict drug sensitivity based on p53 mutation status

suggested_experiments: Thermal shift assay (DSF) to validate R248W impact on protein stability
HDX-MS to map conformational changes induced by R248W
Co-crystallization with APR-246 metabolite to validate binding mode
Cellular assays for p53 transcriptional activity restoration
